accomplished and indicate in the “Acceptance” portion of the
AIR the date of acceptance and put a check (✔) mark whether
the delivery is complete or partial as to quantity, and the
inspection officer to indicate in the “Inspection” portion of the
AIR the date of inspection and put a check (✔) mark indicating
that he has inspected, verified and found in order the items
delivered as to quantity and specifications; and
3.23.4 Create an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of
inventories and prepare the RPCI to report on their existence and
condition.

4.23 We recommended that Management initiate the compliance with the
guidelines and procedures for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE account
balances by establishing an Inventory Committee to oversee and conduct
the process.

4.24 We also recommended that Management:

4.24.1 Direct the Head of the Inventory Committee to complete the
conduct of the physical count of all PPE, prepare the RPCPPE
and facilitate the reconciliation of the result of the inventory count
with property and accounting records and submit a copy of the
report to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year;

4.24.2 Require the Property Custodian to:
4.24.2.1 Maintain Property Cards for all PPE of the
Municipality;
4.24.2.2 Review and update PARs as required, ensuring
inclusion of essential details such as serial numbers,
brand, model and property numbers for proper
identification;

4.24.3 Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
4.24.3.1 Update all Property Ledger Cards and reconcile
records with the Property Custodian;
4.24.3.2 Make the necessary adjustments in the books to correct
the accounting errors on the misclassification of some
PPE items to reflect the correct balances of the affected
accounts in the financial statements;
4.24.3.3 Determine the composition/details of the PPE with a
total book value of ₱979,219.60 for the proper provision
of their depreciation cost;
4.24.3.4 Reconstruct the PPE lapsing schedule using available
source documents to ensure accurate asset monitoring
and financial reporting, and ensure a reliable data
backup system to safeguard critical financial records;
4.24.3.5 Observe proper accounting for semi-expendable
properties using the prescribed new accounts;
4.24.3.6 Record the necessary reclassifying journal entries to
correct accounting errors for proper presentation of the
semi-expendables expense accounts in the financial
statements; and
4.24.3.7 Record the necessary retrospective adjustments for the
reclassification of items recorded under the PPE
account that fall below the ₱50,000.00 threshold based
on the provided schedule of the Audit Team.

5.7 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant,
in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, to submit the required
documents for the purchase of land to facilitate the transfer of its
ownership in the name of the Municipality to fully protect its interest.

6.9 We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant
to:

6.9.1 Determine the nature of the payables and to establish the
existence of valid obligations;
6.9.2 Verify with their counterpart NGAs the amount appearing in the
schedule of Due to NGAs account through confirmation letters to
reconcile the balances appearing in the books and make
adjustments as necessary, and submit the same to the Audit
Team;
6.9.3 Determine the nature of other payables and reclassify the same to
their appropriate accounts to correct the balances of the affected
accounts and attach the relevant documents and analysis of the
accounts to the JEV/s for any adjustment/s made and submit the
same to the Audit Team;
6.9.4 Analyze and verify all the funds recorded under the Due to NGAs
account and if the same were found to be utilized, prepare
liquidation reports and other supporting documents to support
the journal entries in the books of accounts for the liquidation of
the fund transfer and submit the same to the Audit Team for
evaluation; and
6.9.5 Verify and account for the negative balances and make the proper
adjustment to correct the amount presented in the financial
statements and submit the same to the Audit Team.

7.7 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant
to submit the supporting documents of those transactions recorded in the
Accounts Payable account totaling ₱22,744,871.05.

8.12 We recommended that Management provide the needed logistic support
(individuals/ personnel with accounting competencies) to the Accounting
Office to enable the Municipal Accountant to address the issues
concerning the proper preparation, keeping and updating of books of
accounts of the Municipality and timely submission of financial reports.
Further, allocate resources to hire more accounting staff and invest in
training personnel to ensure accuracy and compliance with reporting
standards while improving efficiency through automation.

8.13 Furthermore, we recommended that Management require the:
8.13.1 Municipal Accountant and other concerned department heads to
strictly observe the submission of accounts within the
reglementary period;
8.13.2 Municipal Accountant to:
8.13.2.1 Prepare and maintain SLs and submit immediately to the
Audit Team for verification; and
8.13.2.2 See to it that in the succeeding years, year-end financial
statements and other financial reports are submitted on or
before February 14, for timely communication to
Management of any reportable deficiencies.

9.13 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant
to:

9.13.1 Verify the discrepancies noted, reconcile the balances in the
affected reports, and effect corrections, if necessary, in order to
reflect the correct amount in the financial statements, and furnish
the Audit Team copy of the JEV/s for any adjustments made;
9.13.2 Properly present the adjusted balances of each affected
component of net assets/ equity by restating the corresponding
prior year balances of the affected accounts; and
9.13.3 Prepare the related schedules of the above-mentioned accounts
and observe the disclosure requirements under Paragraph 127 of
IPSAS 1 in the preparation of financial statements.

9.14 We further recommended that Management direct the Municipal
Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, to
prepare reconciliation between actual amounts presented on a
comparable basis in the budget execution report and the Statement of
Financial Performance.

9.15 No written reply was submitted by the concerned departments; however,
during the exit conference, the accounting personnel and the Municipal
Budget Officer acknowledged the audit observations and expressed
compliance with the audit recommendations. Nevertheless, the Audit Team
emphasized the importance of addressing the identified deficiencies.

10.6 We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
prepare and submit immediately the BRS for the month of December
2024 and the succeeding BRS together with the corresponding bank
statements to determine all the reconciling items as of a given period and
promptly adjust the same in order to reconcile the balance per bank with
the accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) review the Journal
Entry Voucher in taking up transfer of funds to ensure that all transactions are
accurately recorded in the books of accounts. Moreover, prepare the necessary
adjustment to reflect the correct balances for the reciprocal accounts.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following to the
Management:
a. For the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Assessor and the
Municipal Treasurer to take necessary measures to update the Report on Real
Property Assessment and the Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s
index card, respectively; and
b. For the MAO to draw journal entry voucher to record the RPT and SET
receivables, make necessary adjustments as warranted and observe the
prescribed accounting procedures in recording RPT and SET receivables and
deferred income accounts accordingly.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following to the
Management:
a) Through the MAO to evaluate dormant or long inactive CIP accounts and draw
a JEV to effect the necessary journal entries to adjust said accounts and the
necessary adjustments to correct equity account relative to the depreciation of
the reclassified accounts;
b) Through the Disposal Committee to observe the necessary protocols in the
disposal of unserviceable properties as stipulated in Joint Circular No. 2024-1
of COA and DBM;
c) Through the GSO in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office to
prepare, maintain and submit to the Municipal Accountant local road network
property cards to facilitate subsequent reconciliation with the Report on
Physical Count of Road Network System; and
d) Through the MAO to adequately disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements
the Road Network System.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following to the
Management:
a) Coordinate closely to expedite the transfer of unutilized, unexpended, or
unobligated balances from current appropriations to the STF, in order to
facilitate the timely and accurate recording of such transfers in both the General
Fund and Trust Fund books before the end of the year;
b) Adhere strictly to the accounting, reporting, and full disclosure requirements
for the LDRRMF as mandated by the aforementioned regulations; and
c) Prioritize the timely submission of the monthly and annual Reports on Sources
and Utilization of the LDRRMF to the audit team and aforesaid government
over sight offices.

We recommended for the management to maximize the
utilization of GAD funds through full implementation of GAD-related programs and
projects in order to attain the objective for which funds were provided. Evaluate the
underlying factors that led to the underutilization and to formulate strategies that will
ensure more efficient and effective use of resources in future planning.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following to the
Management:
a. Thoroughly review the required attachments embodied in government
procurement law and COA Circular 2009-001. A transmittal letter detailing the
list of documents shall be provided and the same be index-tabbed for easy-
reference;
b. Submit the contract documents in its original form (two sets) as these are
subject to review and inspection by the COA Regional Office XII Technical Audit
Group (TAG), when warranted; and
c. Observe the timeline for submission of contracts, POs and supporting
documents for review, evaluation and inspection of projects, programs and
activities (PPAs) implemented by the Agency.

observations.recommendation:
1.10 We recommended that Management take full opportunity on the
application of the prescribed guidelines and procedures for the one-
time cleansing of PPE accounts to come up with reliable PPE balances
that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

1.11 We also recommended that Management:

a. Require the Property Custodian to:
• Strictly comply with the required physical count of all
properties at least once a year using the proper format and
submit a copy of the said report to the Audit Team not later
than January 31 of each year;
• Maintain Property Cards for all PPE of the LGU;
• Review all the PARs issued and cause the renewal thereof when
necessary, especially those that were issued three years ago; and
• Provide relevant details in the previously issued PARs and
succeeding issuances thereof, particularly, the serial numbers,
brand, model and property numbers of properties issued to
end-users.

b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Update all PPE Ledger Cards and perform reconciliation with
the records of the Property Custodian; and
• Make the necessary adjusting journal entries for the noted
accounting errors to reflect the correct balances of the affected
accounts in the financial statements.

2.7 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget
Officer to refrain from obligating and charging prior year’s
expenditures against the current year’s appropriation. Proper
coordination must be observed among municipal officials and
employees in order that expenses are obligated and taken up as
Accounts Payable in the current year and are paid in the succeeding
year.

3.5 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Engineer to
follow up the As-Built Plan with the DPWH and ask for the assistance
of the Project Engineer of the agency to conduct an ocular inspection of
the road network projects to verify their existence.

3.6 We further recommended that Management require the Municipal
Accountant to prepare the adjusting journal entries to take up the
transfer of the asset from the DPWH once the projects are inspected and
accepted by the LGU.

4.7 We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that no additional
cash advance is granted if the previous cash advance is not
liquidated/accounted by issuing a certification that the previous
cash advance was liquidated and accounted for in the books;
b. Require the former officials and employees of the LGU to
immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances through
the issuance of demand letters by the Municipal Accountant;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to issue demand letters
through the office of the officials and employees of the national
government agencies with unliquidated cash advances;
d. Require the Municipal Treasurer to withhold the salaries of
officials and employees whose cash advances remained
unliquidated;
e. Exhaust all possible means to collect the cash advances from
previous employees who are no longer in the service, otherwise,
proceed against the signatories of the clearance who are still in
the service;
f. Request authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit
for cash advances aged 10 years or more and which payees are
already deceased and the probability of collection/liquidation
is remote; and
g. Require the officers and employees to refrain from drawing
cash advances other than travel.

5.7 We recommended that Management ensure compliance with the
settlement of disallowances that had become final and executory and
for which the corresponding COE was issued by requiring the
Municipal Treasurer to withhold the salaries or other compensation
due to the employees liable in satisfaction of the disallowance and
settlement thereof.

5.8 We also recommended that Management ensure that employees who
are retiring or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the
disallowances and charges for which they are liable before the issuance
of clearance from money, property and legal accountability.

6.6 We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Immediately adjust the stale checks amounting to ₱423,272.69
to Accounts Payable;
b. Verify the deposits in transit and trace back their origin by
obtaining supporting documents, such as deposit slips together
with the official receipts, or transaction records for proper
verification of the details of the deposit and make the necessary
adjustments in the accounts affected;
c. Reconcile the cash balance and determine the valid book
reconciling items and immediately prepare adjusting journal
entries; and
d. Prepare and submit the BRS to the Audit Team within the
reglementary period.

7.8 We recommended that Management:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the
Financial Statements the reasons of occurrence of discrepancy
between the actual amounts and final budget, and to trace the
details of the difference under MOOE arising from the
reconciliation between the SCBAA and the SFPe; and

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer
to maintain RAAOs and SAAOB, respectively, to support the
actual amounts in the SCBAA and the reconciliation with the
amounts presented in the SFPe.

8.4 We recommended that Management prepare the related schedules of
the above-mentioned accounts and observe the disclosure requirements
under Paragraph 127 of IPSAS 1 in the preparation of financial
statements.

9.7 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to strictly observe the submission of accounts within the
reglementary period to see to it that in the succeeding years, year-end
financial statements and other financial reports are submitted on or
before February 14 for timely communication to Management of
reportable deficiencies noted.

10.4 We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant
to prepare and maintain General and Subsidiary Ledgers within the
reglementary period.

10.5 We also recommended that Management provide the needed logistic
support (individuals/ personnel with accounting competencies) to the
accounting office to enable the Municipal Accountant to address the
issues concerning the proper preparation, keeping and updating of
books of accounts of the LGU and timely submission of financial
reports. Further, allocate resources to hire more accounting staff and
invest in training personnel to ensure accuracy and compliance with
reporting standards while improving efficiency through automation.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following to the
Management:
a. For the Local Chief Executive to direct the concerned department/personnel in
ensuring full implementation of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances,
draw necessary accounting entries, measurements, presentation and disclosure
requirements as provided in the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020
and ensure completeness of details in the Report on the Physical Count of Property,
Plant, & Equipment (RPCPPE).
b. For the Inventory Committee to evaluate recorded items below the capitalization
criteria whether these are semi-expendable items misclassified as PPE which shall
have been expensed upon issuance or these are items with individual values below
threshold.
For the Municipal Accountant to improve practices in reporting of accounts by:
c. Properly observe the required five percent (5%) residual value across all classes of
items in the PPE for uniformity, unless there is sufficient reason for the LGU to apply
a different percentage in computing for the residual value of a particular item or class
of items in the PPE based on the nature of its operations.
d. Draw a journal entry voucher to effect the necessary entries to adjust PPE items
relative to the required salvage value, the necessary adjustments to correct the
misclassified PPE accounts and reclassification of semi-expendable items with costs
not exceeding capitalization threshold upon coordination with the Inventory
Committee.

We recommended the following to the management:
To the Municipal Accounting Office:
a. Examine the transactions regarding bank account no. 2182-1068-63 to remove the
closed bank account from the financial statements for the current reporting period.
This adjustment should be made promptly to avoid any further misrepresentation in
the Municipality’s financial position.
b. Prepare the necessary journal entry to exclude/adjust the amount of P435,232.77
from the Cash in Bank balance and furnish the Audit Team with the necessary
records and documents to validate the adjustments, if any.
c. Prepare the adjusting journal entry for the minor discrepancies mentioned in the
second observation above.
d. Conduct a detailed review of the bank reconciliation process and implement
measures to enhance accuracy and transparency in the amounts reported in the
financial statements. Strengthen the internal controls to help mitigate similar
discrepancies in the future.
e. Comply with the requirements mentioned above on the conduct of monthly
reconciliation to lessen the accumulation of unreconciled items.
To the Municipal Treasury Office:
f. Take immediate steps to rectify any deficiency and ensure that all cash transactions
are properly documented and recorded in the cashbook.
g. Provide training and capacity building to the person responsible for maintaining the
cashbook. Ensure adequate training on the importance of accurate record-keeping
and proficiency in the established procedures for maintaining the cashbook.
h. Regularly monitor the cashbook maintenance process and conduct periodic reviews
to verify the accuracy of cash transactions and compliance with established
procedures.
i. Submit the data on balances at year end of all the cashbooks maintained.

We recommended the following to the Management:
To the LDRRM Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant:
a. Collaborate to fast-track the transfer of the unutilized/unexpended/unobligated
balances of the prior year appropriations to the Special Trust Fund and incorporate
in the preparation of DRRMP.
To the Municipal Accountant:
b. Monitor the unexpended balances to be recognized under the Trust Liabilities-DRRM
account including its utilization and balances. Ensure the accounting and reporting
guidelines are adhered to, based on COA Circular 2012-002 and RA 10121; and
c. Strictly observe the accounting, reporting and full disclosure requirements of Local
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) pursuant to the aforesaid
regulations.
To the LDRRM Officer:
d. Prioritize the timely submission of the monthly and annual Reports on Sources and
Utilization of the LDRRMF to the audit team and to the respective government
oversight offices such as OCD, DILG, RDRRMC and DBM.

We further recommended that Management require the former Municipal

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the error in recognizing

with total contract costs of ₱24,991,409.26 with the following proposed
adjusting journal entries:
Accounts Payable 24,991,409.26
CIP–Buildings and Other Structures 24,991,409.26

We recommended that Management require:
4.12.1 The Municipal Treasurer to maintain the equivalent property cards
for PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and
the Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its
components;

Instruct the Acting Municipal Accountant to maintain accounting records on the dollar account under the name of the LGU and reflect its peso value in the financial statements with appropriate disclosures.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to immediately document the bank and book reconciling items that remained uncleared since CY 2010 by analyzing the affected transactions and to request from the bank concerned documents to support  the adjustments in the books; and prepare the corresponding adjustments to present the correct balance of the CIB account and to prepare and submit immediately the monthly BRS together with the corresponding bank statements to determine all the reconciling items as of a given period and promptly adjust the same.
We further recommended that Management instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to require in writing the former Municipal Treasurers to provide the deposit slips of the various DITs; otherwise, cause the refund of the total amount of the long outstanding DIT.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to issue Official Receipt to acknowledge the receipt of fund from the GF and to record the adjustments in the Cashbook – Cash in Treasury and reconcile daily the cash on hand with the balances in the Cashbook – Cash in Treasury.
We also recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the receipt of Fund Transfer  to the GF from the TF in the amount of ₱54,124.02 and to correct the over deposit of ₱800.00 to bring the Cash – Local Treasury account in the TF to its normal balance.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the cost of the completed Boardwalk at Marine Protected Area and Manpower Development Center Phase III to the appropriate asset accounts supported with required documents and to provide the depreciation costs on the said assets in the books of accounts for CY 2020.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to maintain the equivalent property cards for PPE to account for the receipt and disposition of the same and the Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components  and for the Acting Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, to include in the Local Road Network Ledger Cards the information on the type of component, dimensions and maintenance history of the road networks which are necessary to support the accuracy and reliability of the balances of PPE accounts in the financial statements.
We also recommended that Management require the Head of the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all its PPE and to prepare the inventory report to facilitate the reconciliation of the result of the inventory count with property and accounting records maintained by the Acting Municipal Treasurer and Acting Municipal Accountant, respectively, consistent with the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to obtain the supporting documents/records and details of the financial liabilities concerned in order to provide its adequate disclosures or relevant information in the financial statements and to notify the payees concerned of the existence of the obligation.
We also recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry to correct the error in recording payments of consultancy services previously recognized as accounts payable.
The suggested journal entry is as follows:
Accounts Payable			₱ 105,000.00
Consultancy Services 	 	   ₱ 105,000.00

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to transfer and record on time the unexpended balances of the QRF and DRRMF – MOOE to the STF in the TF Books.
We also recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to correct the overstatement of transfer of funds in the amount of ₱11,600.00 and to cause the reversion of the unutilized DRRMF for CY 2014 amounting to ₱716,474.03 to the GF books to properly present the Trust Liabilities – DRRM account in the financial statements as of year-end.

We recommended that Management:
Require the MBO to submit the Sangguniang Panlalawigan Resolutions on the review of the Supplemental Appropriations;
Require the BAC, in coordination with the BAC Secretary, to include the warranty clause in the Contract and POs for Infrastructure Projects and Goods to be procured using the LDRRMF and MDF and to require the submission of the mandatory requirements from the Suppliers any time before award of contract or after award but before payment in accordance with Item 4.2 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020 to protect the financial interest of the LGU; and
Require the Acting Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the BAC, LDRRMO and Municipal Health Officer, to submit the mandatory requirements, list of affected families and proof of receipt of distribution of rice, medicines and other relief goods and other supporting documents for further evaluation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management require the MSWDO, in coordination with the Acting Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer, to submit the authorization letters and photocopies of valid ID of authorized representative of the 41 individuals who received the payments and photocopies of the valid ID of social pensioners to support the propriety of transactions.

We recommended that Management:
Require the BAC and BAC Secretariat to invite representative from specific relevant chamber-member of the Philippine Chamber of Commerce and Industry as observer in all stages of the procurement, require from the supplier the submission of Income Tax Return for the year 2019, post the NOA in the PhilGEPS within three days from its issuance, disclose in the certifications of posting the NOA and NTP to conspicuous places the date of posting thereto and issue the NTP within seven days from the approval of the contract;
Require the BAC with the assistance of the Technical Working Group (TWG) Members to include in the Post-Qualification Evaluation Report actions taken in the verification of delay in partial delivery for ongoing government and private contracts, bidder’s failure to deliver or perform any or all of the goods or services within the period specified in the contract and unsatisfactory performance of the supplier’s obligation as per contract terms and conditions at the time of inspection; and
Require the Acting Municipal Accountant and the BAC to submit the lacking documents for further evaluation of the Audit Team.
We further recommended that Management ensure the completion of the MRF within the target date to enable the utilization of the said Solid Waste Management Equipment to its intended purpose and to provide adequate storage to safeguard the said equipment against elements causing rapid deterioration.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MBO, to submit the SB Resolutions on the implementation of the 11 projects totaling ₱746,424.07. We also recommended that Management require the MBO to see to it that obligations for PPAs described in generic terms are supported with SB Resolutions authorizing the LCE to enter in contract and specifying the programs thereon before certifying the existence of available appropriation in the ObR.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the HRMO, cause the refund of the amount paid in excess of the equivalent amount of the number of days specified in their respective JO Contract in compliance with the  existing terms and conditions in the JO/contract in the payment of services rendered by the JO Workers.
We also recommended that Management ensure in their future transactions that all terms and conditions in the hiring of JO Workers and payment of their services are covered in the JO/contract to support payment of claims for wages.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to monitor the remittance of collections of all collecting officers and the sequential issuance of the official receipts by verifying the RCD, checking the statement of accountable forms as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issuance and the ending balances on hand, making physical count of the accountable forms remaining in the custody of the collectors and comparing the same against the ending balance on hand before affixing his signature at the back of the triplicate copy of the last official receipt issued. We also recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to count the money turned over to him before signing the certification and receipt portion in the RCD to provide adequate safeguard of the government funds against possible loss or misuse.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to assign the collection functions to permanent employees of the LGU in order to provide adequate safeguard of government resources against the risk of loss or misuse of government funds.

We recommended that Management require the designated employee or official in the issuance of DTT to see to it that the required information is duly indicated before issuance thereof and to oblige the driver of each government vehicle to transcribe or summarize all the trip tickets issued during the month in the Monthly Report of Official Travels in chronological order to support the reasonableness and propriety of gasoline expenses.
We also recommended that Management require the MBO to avoid charging prior year expenses on gasoline to the current year appropriations by requiring all officials and employees to submit their claims before year-end to obligate funds before reversion.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to see to it that all claims for travel expenses are supported with duly approved travel orders and are incurred in the performance of the official functions of the Municipal officials and employees before affixing his signature in the DV and to pay the equivalent cost of the customary mode of transportation in cases the vehicles used are not owned by the LGU.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to submit the supporting documents to the payments of RATA of Municipal officials and employees observing an eight-hour workday such as approved DTR and Certification issued by the Office of the HRMO showing the number of workdays of actual performance in a month for further evaluation of the Audit Team. We also recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to compute the RATA of the members of the local sanggunian based on actual work/attendance of the official vis-à-vis the work schedule or performance standards prescribed by law or by competent authority in accordance with the provisions of LBC No. 103 dated May 15, 2013 and to cause the refund of the excess payment in the amount of ₱55,037.50.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to indicate in the RFQs at what stage of the procurement process the supplier/contractor will submit the documentary requirements prior to the issuance of NOA, to require the suppliers to submit the mandatory documents in the use of alternative method of Small Value Procurement to ensure that the most advantageous price to the LGU are obtained from suppliers of known qualifications, and to instruct the Acting Municipal Accountant to see to it that all of the documentary requirements are complete to ensure transparency and propriety of the procurement transactions.

We recommended that Management ensure strict observance with the provisions of Section 343 of RA No. 7160 by requiring the MBO to obligate the charges for meals and snacks during various meetings, sessions and reception to visitors for various activities in the representation allowance of the office concerned and impose control measures, in coordination with the Acting Municipal Accountant, to limit the expenses on meals and snacks.

We recommended that the Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the Acting Municipal Treasurer, to submit the DVs, official receipts, payrolls, JEVs and the Trial Balance and Financial Statements within the prescribed period for timely examination and verification of financial transactions.

We recommended that Management require the BAC, with the assistance of the BAC Secretariat, to submit immediately the contracts and supporting documents of nine projects with total contract cost of ₱40,955,206.35 to the Audit Team and to observe the prescribed period of submission of five (5) working days from the execution of a contract in order to gain the benefits of facilitating the review and evaluation process and obtaining suggested corrective measures on the defects and deficiencies noted in the contract and its supporting documents which are relevant in decision making.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Engineer or the inspectorate team, if any, to make preliminary inspection of the infrastructure projects and submit a punch-list to the contractor as provided in Item 7, Annex E, of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in order to inform the contractor of the remaining works, work deficiencies for necessary corrections, and the specific duration/time to fully complete the project considering the approved remaining contract time.
We further recommended that Management require the contractor to complete immediately the unperformed concrete works and impose liquidated damages and other necessary measures. Otherwise, require the contractor to refund the equivalent cost of the unperformed concrete works amounting to ₱164,735.30.

We recommended that the Management require the Inspection Officer, in coordination with the Property Officer, to verify and inspect carefully all purchases made by the agency to ensure its conformity with specifications provided in the PO before acceptance and to require the supplier to replace the delivered reconditioned Multi-Purpose Truck with a unit in conformity with the specifications indicated in the PO.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to adopt effective collection system by sending notice to delinquent taxpayers and requesting the assistance of barangay officials to ensure collection of delinquent RPT.
We also recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer issue demand letters to market stall holders to settle their unpaid rental fees and electric bills to provide additional funds to support the operations of the program of LGU geared towards the welfare of the constituents.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to exert more effort in requiring the constituents/borrowers concerned to settle their obligations by issuing demand letters and Statement of Accounts to increase the collection of loans receivable and generate funds to extend the livelihood program to other constituents of the LGU.
We also recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MSWDO, to submit the following documents to enable the Audit Team to review and evaluate the nature of the fund transfer from the national government and loan agreement between the LGU and loan borrower, to wit:
Copy of Memorandum of Agreement between the LGU and the source agency and other supporting documents; and
Copy of Loan Agreement.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to identify the specific gender issues caused by the unequal situation between women and men in the locality in the GAD Plan and Budget and to identify PPAs that are responsive to these gender issues in order to attain the main intentions in promoting gender-responsive governance, protection and fulfilment of women’s human rights and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment.
We further recommended that Management, through the GFPS, prioritize in the succeeding GAD Plan and Budget the recurring/emerging gender issues in the LGU, results of gender assessment of the organization or its PPAs and other GAD PPAs identified in the GAD Code of the LGU that have not been considered in the previous year to achieve a more effective GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to cause the immediate remittance of the premiums/contributions to the government agencies concerned.
We also recommended that Management ensure timely remittance of premiums to GSIS by requiring the HRMO to prepare and transmit promptly the required forms in updating the records of the personnel to the GSIS to update the personal records of employees both in the LGU and GSIS.

We recommended that Management direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to remit fully the amount of taxes due and all subsequent internal revenue taxes withheld to the BIR within the prescribed period to support the government’s efforts in intensifying the collection of revenues and avoid the imposition of fines and penalties provided in the said Revenue Regulation.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide a complete and detailed schedule of depreciation in compliance with IPSAS Nos. 17 and 66 and Section III (11) of COA Circular No. 2015-008 to support the accuracy and reliability of the Depreciation Expenses and Accumulated Depreciation accounts presented in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:
Create an Inventory Team that will conduct the required physical count of all properties at least once a year and to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to facilitate the reconciliation of the balance of the PPE with Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards to ensure accuracy of the PPE account presented in the financial statements;
Require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to maintain complete and updated Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, as required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPE and to facilitate identification thereof; and
Submit to the Audit Team the RPCPPE in compliance with Section 124 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to provide a complete and detailed schedule of depreciation in compliance with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) Nos. 17 and 66 and Section III (11) of COA Circular No. 2015-008 to support the accuracy and reliability of the Depreciation Expenses and Accumulated Depreciation accounts presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the employees concerned to prepare and submit the Report of Local Road Network and Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network in order to facilitate full transition of transfer of Local Road Networks in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 to ensure accuracy of the Road Network account presented in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Municipal Accountant to immediately document the book and bank reconciling items that remained uncleared since CY 2010 by analyzing the affected transactions and request from the bank concerned documents to support the adjustments in the books; and prepare the corresponding adjustments in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the Cash in Bank account balance and fair presentation in the FS.
We further recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Treasurer to provide the corresponding deposit slip of the various deposits in transit (DIT); otherwise, cause the refund of the total amount of the long outstanding DIT.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the provisions of Section 59, Volume I of Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) Manual for LGUs and:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to send a written notice to payees of the existence of checks at least one month before it becomes stale. Consequently, for those unclaimed checks that become stale in her custody, return the original DV and supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books;
Require the Municipal Accountant, upon receipt of the original DV and supporting documents of the stale checks, to prepare Journal Entry Voucher to record the transaction as Accounts Payable; and
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to process the replacement of stale checks in the hands of the payee upon submission of the stale checks to the Treasury Office.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements together with the corresponding bank statements within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 74 of P.D. 1445 and Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-001 dated October 2, 1996 to ensure timely verification and reconciliation of the balances of Cash in Bank account of the Municipality with the depository bank for fair presentation of the account balance in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with the provision of Section 74 of P.D. 1445 by instructing the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements using the Cash in Bank balances recorded in the General Ledger and effect immediately in the book of accounts the necessary adjustments with supporting documents to present the accurate balance of the Cash in Bank account in the Financial Statements.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain accounting records on the dollar account under the name of the Local Government Unit and reflect its peso value in the Financial Statements with appropriate disclosures.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all collecting officers to remit their collections to the Treasurer’s Office within the prescribed period in compliance with Section 29, Volume 1 of the NGAS Manual to safeguard the government funds against possible loss or misuse. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer closely monitor the remittance of collections by the collecting officers and ensure that no delay would be incurred considering the distance of the Treasury Office from the place of their assignment.

We recommended that the Agency Head provide adequate safeguard of government resources against the risk of loss or misuse of government funds by assigning the collection functions to the permanent employees as revenue collectors of the Municipality.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with the provision of Section 2 of P.D. 1445 by instructing the Municipal Accountant to analyze the affected transactions and to prepare the corresponding adjustments, if any; otherwise, demand for the refund of overpayment/remittances from the Financial Institutions. We also recommended the Municipal Accountant maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SL) for the Other Payables account in compliance with Section 10, Volume II of Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs to provide adequate monitoring tool that will ensure the accuracy of remittances of contributions and loan payments to the financial institutions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445; and to instruct the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents prescribed in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445; and to instruct the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to submit the required documents prescribed in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with the requirements prescribed in Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and cause the submission of copies of contracts to the Audit Team to enable the auditor to conduct timely review and technical evaluation of the contracts.
Further, take appropriate administrative disciplinary action as provided in Section 4 of the same Circular against the responsible officer concerned for unjustified non-compliance with the circular.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with the provisions of Section 22 of RA 7160 by submitting the authorization issued by the Sangguniang Bayan for the implementation of the projects identified in the schedule; otherwise, the transactions will be disallowed in audit pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012. It was further recommended that the PPAs be carefully formulated and specified in the Annual Budget and Annual Investment Plan (AIP) if Management wishes to avoid the necessity of authorization of the Local Sangguniang Bayan for every implementation of the PPAs.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with Section 5.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 by maximizing the utilization of the 20% Development Fund allocated for the implementation of various projects, programs and activities in order to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure that all Projects/Programs/Activities (PPAs) charged against the 20% Development Fund are in consonance with the provisions of Sections 2.0 and 3.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 towards the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with Section 3, paragraph 3 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 and Sections 5.1.2 and 5.1.3 dated COA Circular No. 2012-002 by requiring the LDRRMO to include the list of projects and activities to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years to ensure implementation of projects and activities for optimal utilization of the fund.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with Section 5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 by instructing the Municipal Accountant to record in a timely and accurate manner the unexpended balances of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (DRRMF) – MOOE to the Special Trust Fund in the Trust Fund books to ensure fair representation of the Trust Liabilities – DRRM account in the financial statements at year-end.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure strict compliance with the procurement provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 and the fundamental principle on financial transactions prescribed in Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445; and instruct the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to comment/justify on the deficiencies observed, and to submit the required documents prescribed in conformity with COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) ensure compliance with Sections 2 and 16 of RA No. 9003 by instructing the employees concerned to expedite completion and approval of the revised 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan in order to have concrete guidelines that will be adopted for an efficient implementation of programs and activities for the segregation, collection, transport, storage, processing, treatment and disposal of solid waste towards ensuring protection of public health and its environment.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive engage the services of consultants only when the relevant experience, level of expertise or attention required for the government project or related activities is beyond the optimum in-house capability of the LGU and not redundant to the duties and functions of the regular employees in compliance with the provisions of Annex “B”, General Principles of Consulting Services, of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) and COA Circular No. 2012-003, to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.
It was also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the payments made to the Consultant recorded as Honorarium to Consultancy Services as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009 to present fairly the balances of the expense accounts in the Financial Statements, and for the officials and employees concerned to submit the lacking documents pursuant to Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 for further evaluation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure compliance with the provisions of Section 7 of the Rules and Regulation in the Settlement of Accounts; and cause the settlement of Notice of Disallowances that has already become final and executory and for which the corresponding COA Order of Execution has been issued. Effect the withholding of salaries for the settlement of disallowances; and in addition, initiate the necessary administrative and/or criminal action in case of unjustified failure/refusal to effect compliance with the Order.

Checks maintained and issued by the Municipality on its various bank accounts were not included in the preparation of monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms which affected the accuracy of the said report.
The Manual on New Government Accounting System specifically provides that “The Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms shall be used to report the quantity of accountable forms received, issued or cancelled by the accountable officer as of the end of the month.”
The Handbook on Cash Examination issued by the Commission on Audit on November 2002 specifically defines Accountable Forms as:
Accountable Forms- Accountable forms are serially pre-numbered forms and/or forms with fixed amount or official documents like Import Entry Declaration for which the custodian thereof shall be accountable.
Accountable forms without money value
Official Receipts
Checks
Treasury Warrants
Money Orders
Cattle Registration Certificates
Marriage Licenses
Driver’s Licenses
Motor vehicle registration receipts
Other specifically designed form(s)
Accountable forms with money value
Postage Stamps
Cash Tickets
Documentary Stamps
Money Orders
The Office of the Municipal Treasurer prepares the monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms. However, during the cash examination of the Accountable Officer/ Municipal Treasurer checks maintained and issued by the Municipality on its various bank accounts were not reflected in the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms, thus affecting the accuracy of the report issued since the information provided was incomplete.
The non-conformance with the guidelines prescribed in the Handbook on Cash Examination for the preparation and submission of Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms (RAAF) casted doubts on the accuracy of the submitted report.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer informed  that her office has started to comply with the said recommendation.
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to include the inventory of checks in the preparation of the Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms in order to prepare an accurate and complete report.

dated February 12, 2009 to enable the auditor to conduct a systematic and effective
review process with a view of generating timely and relevant audit results.

We recommended that management:
 Formulate programs, projects and activities that are gender related to
conform with the purpose of establishing the GAD Plan, that is, to
address gender issues and women empowerment.
 Likewise, establish and maintain a GAD Database in implementing
the Magna Carta for Women which helps in formulating GAD-related
activities based on information gathered.
 Prepare the Annual GAD Accomplishment Report as prescribed
under DILG-NEDA Joint Circular No. 2013-01 which is also a
requirement for the conduct of GAD Audit.

We recommended that management establish their own sanitary landfill as
required in the above provisions of law to improve the existing dump site in
addressing common solid waste management problems and/or establishing common
waste disposal facilities of the municipality.

We recommended that management comply with the mandatory
requirement of providing a separate allocation of one percent (1%) of IRA for the
local councils for the protection of children for strengthening and implementation of
its programs, projects and activities as required under DILG Memorandum No.
2012-120 and Section 15 of RA 9344. Likewise, a separate report on its utilization
should be provided to match the plan and budget against its accomplishments.

We recommended the management that for lump-sum allocation, seek the
authorization from the Sangguniang Bayan for each and every project to support
the authority of its execution in accordance with Section 447 (2) (ii) of R.A. 7160 and
stipulations in the Supreme Court En Banc Decision G.R. No. 175527 dated
December 8, 2008. We also recommended that management carefully formulate and
plan comprehensive projects that can be specifically identified in the Municipal
Development Plan.

We recommended that management ensure that all transactions involving
alternative methods of procurement shall be supported with BAC Resolution
recommending and justifying to the Head of Procuring Entity (HOPE) the use of
alternative method of procurement to be approved by the HOPE, as well as the
approval by the HOPE of the BAC resolution recommending the award of
contract. Likewise, PhilGEPS Registration Number and other eligibility
requirements should be attached in compliance with Section 54.6 of the IRR of
R.A. 9184 and Appendix A of Annex “H” of the same IRR.

We recommended that the management:
 Submit the complete documentary requirements for the conduct of auditorial
review of contract as mentioned in COA Memo Circular No. 2005-027 and
for payment of claims under COA Circular No. 2012-001.
 Strictly adhere to the provision of Section 13 and 39 of the Revised IRR of
RA 9184 to ensure transparency and competitiveness in the procurement
activities.
 Explain in writing why the BAC entered into contract with RP Parayno
Construction despite the inability to post the required minimum amount of
performance securities under Section 39 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
Otherwise, this constitutes an irregular transaction for awarding the contract
with inadequate performance bond.
 Stop the practice of incurring expenditures more than what was received out
of the fund transfer and strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 4 of PD
1145, otherwise similar practice in the future will be dealt with more
severely.

We recommended that management strictly adhere to the provisions of RA
9679 in the computation of employer share to the monthly Pag-IBIG premium
contribution of its employees. Likewise, inform Pag-IBIG officials/officers regarding
the excess remittances made by the agency, and negotiate whether these remittances
can be applied to future payments of their employees Pag-IBIG contributions.

We recommended that management exert more effort in communicating
with its constituents regarding the settlement of their obligations. Require the
Municipal Accountant to issue demand letters to debtors and apply legal sanctions,
as the case maybe. Formulate a collection scheme in order to enforce collection in
order to decrease the uncollectible balance.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Treasurer to
continue exerting efforts in the collection of delinquent stall rental fees. Likewise,
imposed better strategies to avoid incurrence of delinquent or non-collection of
rental fees on due dates.

We reiterated that management require all concerned officials and
employees to apply the appropriate withholding tax rates on all government
purchases in accordance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 to
prevent revenue loss and to avoid possible penalties arising from non-compliance
thereof.

In compliance with the National Internal Revenue Code of 1997, the total amount
of ₱11,102,449.03 was withheld by the municipality for CY 2017 from compensation as
well as from government money payments on goods and services and accordingly
remitted the amount of ₱10,382,293.95 as of December 31, 2017, leaving a balance of
₱720,155.08. However, subsequent remittance in the amount of ₱720,155.08 was made
in January 2018.

We have reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer/General Services Officer as well as the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain reports/records like the RPCI, RPCPPE, Supplies Ledger Cards, Stock Cards and Property Cards for the proper monitoring of properties purchased, donated, transferred or disposed of so that reconciliation of accounting records with the physical count will be completed.

We have reiterated that Management:
Require all Accountable Officers to liquidate their cash advances on time, otherwise, impose administrative sanctions against them for any unjustifiable reasons due to failure to liquidate cash advances within the prescribe period, in accordance with Section 89 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Civil Service Commission No. 0676 dated June 17, 2004.
Strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
Cause or order the withholding of any money claims against the accountable officers concerned until they have fully settled their unliquidated cash advances.
Stop granting cash advances other than traveling expenses to non-bonded officials and employees.
Stop the practice of making cash advances for the regular procurement of goods and services, but instead make a direct payment through checks to the suppliers using competitive bidding, if not warranted, use the alternative mode of procurement.

We have reiterated that management expedite the official creation of the municipality’s Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to effectively manage the response of the LGU during calamities and to ensure that DRRM activities are align with the national efforts.
Include in the funding sources of its LDRRMFIP the unexpended balances of LDRRMF of prior years in order for the LGU to maximize the funding and utilization of the calamity fund. Likewise, we recommend that preparation of LFRRMFIP should follow the format provided in “Annex A” of COA Circular No. 2012-002 specifically classifying the MOOE and CO expenditures. Moreover, management should adjust properly its reports involving changes in the budgetary allocation brought up by sudden significant changes in order to convey correct amounts presented in the financial statements.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant:
To review and correct the entry made in the Trust Fund books in the inclusion of Capital Outlay in the unexpended balance of LDRRMF by reverting it back to the continuing appropriations under the General Fund books as stated in the LDRRMFIP.
To ensure that all unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF for CO shall be made continuing in the General Fund books until the projects funded thereof are completed and any savings shall be available for use in the disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in the LDRRMFIP.
To draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to correct the misclassified financial assistance under “Due to NGAs” and to properly account the balances to its appropriate account, “Trust Liability- DRRM” in the Trust Fund books. Also, submit documents to support the claims for the utilization of the said financial assistance and record it to the books, if any, otherwise, refund the full or unutilized amount.
Ensure observance of the rules set forth under COA Circular No. 2014-002 pertaining to the accounting, inventory and monitoring guidelines for procured resources out of the LDRRMF by appropriately recognizing the procured assets and preparation of inventory documents and reports aforementioned above.

We have recommended that management:
Submit immediately to the Office of Auditor copies of bank statements with corresponding bank reconciliation statements of various bank accounts for verification thereof.
For the large amount of discrepancies/ differences noted in the general and subsidiary ledger balances of the Cash in Bank accounts as compare with the cashbook balances and bank statements/ confirmations, immediate reconciliation should be prepared to reflect the correct amount of Cash in Bank in the financial statements to be led by the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer. Furthermore, submit to the Office of the Auditor the necessary documents and records to support such reconciliation and adjustments made.
Prepare adjustments/ journal entry voucher to take up the Dollar Account in order to properly disclose the said fund in the financial statements of the agency.
Prepare adjustments to the cashbooks and general ledger of the LBP Account No. 1272-1041-70 (Trust Fund-NEDA) in order to properly close the said account and to provide an SB Resolution approving the closing of such accounts in accordance to laws, rules and regulations.

Discontinue the practice of charging prior year’s expenditure to current year appropriations.

We have recommended that management strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. 9184 and its IRR by:

We have reiterated that management ensure that all programs and projects to be included in the priority projects are consistent with the projects/programs contained in the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-01 to attain the desired socio-economic and environmental management outcome. Moreover, management should observe optimum utilization of the development fund as required by the same circular.

b. We have recommended that management strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations by accomplishing projects through complete public bidding in order to protect the best interest of the municipality and avoid suspensions and/or disallowances in audit. We also recommend to the concerned officials to stop the practice of splitting contracts as laid down under Item 54.1 of the IRR of the Government Procurement Act.
observations.managementAction: Management continued to include Sports Development as a priority program of its Local Development Plan.
observations.reason:
Since Sports Development is still included as a priority program under the Local Development Fund, we have included in our audit finding the aforementioned deficiency.

Verification of documents submitted to the Audit Team revealed that some PPAs were still not subjected to complete bidding process. Such deficiencies were summarized in an AOM regarding infractions of RA 9184.

observations.recommendation: We have reiterated that management require the Municipal Treasurer to continue exerting efforts in the collection of delinquent stall rental fees. Likewise, imposed better strategies to avoid incurrence of delinquent or non-collection of rental fees on due dates.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Municipal Treasurer assigned a market officer in-charge of monitoring the demand and collection of delinquent stall rentals.
observations.reason: As per inquiry with the Office of the Municipal Treasurer, delinquent stall rentals as of December 31, 2016 amounted to ₱50,000.00 compared to CY 2015’s balance of ₱83,740.00.
